region_of_origin: Northern and Eastern Europe (Scandinavia, Germany, Russia, Baltic states)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301710 covers rye flour; wholegrain specificity is captured via classification and notes.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for wholegrain rye flour as a food substance; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of wholegrain rye flour used in baking is approximately 30 g (about 3 level tablespoons), providing roughly 97.5 kcal, 4.8 g protein, 20.6 g carbohydrate, and 7.1 g dietary fibre.

nutrient_source.title: Wholegrain Rye Flour — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F05040017 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several micronutrients not reported by HPB are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 20065 (Rye flour, dark) where available; fields not found in either source are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Wholegrain rye flour GI is estimated at approximately 45 (low) based on published GI values for whole rye grain products in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283). Rye bread (wholegrain/pumpernickel) GI values range from 41–55 in the Sydney database; wholegrain rye flour as a raw ingredient is assigned GI 45 as a conservative central estimate. Glycemic load per 30 g serving = (45 × 20.6 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 9.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (2 mg/100 g) and very high potassium (717 mg/100 g) with exceptional dietary fibre (23.8 g/100 g), strongly aligned with DASH diet principles.
hypertension.guidance: Wholegrain rye flour is highly suitable for individuals with hypertension; its sodium-to-potassium ratio is extremely favourable, and its high soluble fibre content supports arterial compliance and blood pressure reduction — however, sodium content in finished baked goods will depend on added salt in the recipe and should be monitored.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycaemic index (~45), exceptional dietary fibre (23.8 g/100 g) including beta-glucans, and low sugar (2.31 g/100 g) collectively slow postprandial glucose absorption and improve insulin sensitivity.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Wholegrain rye flour is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when used with portion control, as its low GI and high fibre content attenuate postprandial glycaemic response; total carbohydrate is 68.6 g/100 g as raw flour, so serving size in baked goods must be managed to avoid excessive carbohydrate load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.29 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, high dietary fibre (23.8 g/100 g), and high potassium (717 mg/100 g) support cardiovascular health through LDL reduction and blood pressure management.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Wholegrain rye flour is cardioprotective as a baking ingredient; its high soluble fibre content reduces LDL cholesterol, its negligible saturated fat and zero trans fat minimise atherogenic risk, and its high potassium supports vascular function — it is consistent with heart-healthy dietary patterns such as the Mediterranean and DASH diets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High dietary fibre (23.8 g/100 g) from a whole-food source supports gut microbiome immune modulation; negligible saturated fat and zero trans fat reduce pro-inflammatory load.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Wholegrain rye flour is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its anti-inflammatory fibre profile; however, rye contains gluten and patients with concurrent coeliac disease or gluten sensitivity — which is more prevalent in autoimmune conditions — should avoid it and consult their clinician.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Wholegrain rye flour is produced by milling the entire rye grain (Secale cereale) including the bran, germ, and endosperm, yielding a coarse-textured, greyish-brown flour with a distinctive tangy, earthy, and slightly sour flavour. Unlike refined rye flour, the wholegrain variety retains all the nutritional components of the original grain, making it one of the most fibre-dense flours available — providing 23.8 g dietary fibre per 100 g, which is exceptional among cereal flours. It is a staple of Northern and Eastern European baking traditions, particularly in Scandinavian, German, and Baltic cuisines, where it is used to produce dense, moist breads such as pumpernickel, rugbrød (Danish rye bread), and sourdough rye loaves. The flour has a low glycaemic index of approximately 45, attributed to its high beta-glucan and arabinoxylan soluble fibre content, which slows gastric emptying and attenuates postprandial glucose response. Nutritionally, it is notable for its high protein content (15.91 g/100 g), very low sodium (2 mg/100 g), high potassium (717 mg/100 g), and significant iron (4.97 mg/100 g) and phosphorus (632 mg/100 g). It is suitable for vegetarians, vegans, and halal and kosher diets, but is not gluten-free and must be avoided by individuals with coeliac disease or gluten intolerance. Clinically, it is well-suited for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease, and is consistent with DASH and Mediterranean dietary patterns. As a raw ingredient, it is always used in baked or cooked preparations and is not consumed directly.
common_user_queries: Is wholegrain rye flour good for diabetics?
What is the glycaemic index of rye flour?
Is rye flour high in fibre?
Can I use rye flour for weight loss?
What is the difference between rye flour and wholegrain rye flour?
Is rye flour good for blood pressure?
How much protein is in rye flour?
Is rye flour suitable for a heart-healthy diet?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05040017, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). Fatty acid fractions and additional micronutrients are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (entry 20065, dark rye flour), which is an approved secondary source and closely matches the wholegrain rye flour profile. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using analogous wholegrain rye bread products, which is the standard approved approach for raw flour GI estimation.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.29 g), monounsaturated fat (0.26 g), polyunsaturated fat (1.07 g), trans fat (0.0 g confirmed), and micronutrients (thiamine, riboflavin, niacin, B6, folate, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, water) are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 20065 (Rye flour, dark) as these fields are not individually reported in the HPB source record. This is documented as an adapted source match. 2. Trans fat is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for dark rye flour. 3. Vitamin C, vitamin A, vitamin B12, and vitamin D are confirmed zero or negligible for wholegrain rye flour per USDA FoodData Central. 4. Cholesterol is confirmed zero (0.0 mg/100 g) per HPB source — this is naturally zero as rye flour is a plant-derived food with no animal components. 5. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for wholegrain rye bread products (GI range 41–55); no direct GI measurement for raw wholegrain rye flour as an ingredient is available in the approved GI database. 6. This food is not cuisine-specific to Singapore, Japan, Korea, Indonesia, Thailand, or Vietnam; therefore no mandatory local-language names apply under G6 multilingual rules. The food is of Northern/Eastern European origin and is used globally as a generic baking ingredient. 7. FoodOn URI FOODON_03301710 and FoodEx2 code A000E are used as defensible ontology anchors; SNOMED CT code is omitted as no verified code for wholegrain rye flour as a food substance was identified. 8. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, and all vitamin/mineral fields supplemented from USDA SR Legacy are noted as adapted values; the HPB source does not individually report these fractions.
